patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 08/13] mm: swap in PMD swap entries as whole THPs during swapoff
Re: [PATCH 2/8] mm: percpu: charge obj_exts allocation with __GFP_ACCOUNT
Re: [PATCH v2] btrfs: use IOMAP_DIO_BOUNCE flag instead of falling back to buffered IO
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch updates Btrfs to use the IOMAP_DIO_BOUNCE flag instead of falling back to buffered IO, which is expected to improve performance. The previous fallback to buffered IO was likely introducing unnecessary overhead due to its synchronous nature and additional work required for page cache management. By using the bounce flag, the patch aims to reduce this overhead and make Btrfs operations more efficient.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the new bounce behavior exposes problems related to page faults during write, specifically a bug in nodatasum mount option generic/362, which can fail due to failed page fault-in and handling of later buffered fallback.
review_comments.raw_body:
I guess it's mostly due to the page cache synchronization.


But please do not be too excited for now, this new bounce behavior 
exposes several problems, mostly related to the page fault during write.

It exposed a bug that even without this patch, for nodatasum mount 
option that generic/362 (*) can still fail due to failed page fault-in 
and handling of later buffered fallback.

*: Needs this patch to reliably trigger the failure, or it will only 
fail with newly formatted TEST_DEV: 
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-btrfs/20260526070055.60193-1-wqu@suse.com/T/#u

Thanks,
Qu

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] btrfs: add message format for qgroupid

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations
[PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem where the defrag_mode in the Linux kernel's page allocator fails to handle non-reclaimable allocations, such as those made with GFP_ATOMIC flags. When defrag_mode is enabled, it enforces ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT to prevent fragmentation, but this causes failures for non-reclaimable allocations that cannot invoke direct reclaim or compaction. The patch clears the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT flag and retries the allocation if it requests kswapd reclaim but cannot do direct reclaim itself. This fix addresses a large number of SLUB allocation failures under network-heavy workloads.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that defrag_mode=1 causes SLUB allocation failures for GFP_ATOMIC allocations under network-heavy workloads, agreeing that the issue is significant enough to warrant a fix.
review_comments.raw_body:
We observed it on a few of the Meta workloads that adopted
defrag_mode=1.

For the service under load there were 85509 SLUB allocation failures
messages in dmesg within 2 hours. All of them are GFP_ATOMIC allocations
for skbuff_head_cache, despite free pages being available in other
migratetype freelists (~13 GB free).

Since it is networking path from the practical point of view, this means
dropped packets, failed RPC requests, tail latency spikes and overall
service degradation.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned the urgency of the patch, suggesting it may not be necessary to include it in the upcoming kernel release (v7.1) due to its relatively low failure rate.
review_comments.raw_body:
OK, thanks.   I assume 12 failures per second isn't a disaster, and that
there's no need to fast-track this into 7.1?

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Vlastimil (SUSE) requested more information about the usage and motivations behind defrag_mode, specifically asking for insights on which workloads benefit from it and what are the expectations.
review_comments.raw_body:
Do you (or Johannes) have some observations to share about what
motivated those to adopt it, what kind of workloads benefit and how?
Because I have no idea who uses this mode and what are the expectations.

Thanks,
Vlastimil

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that defrag_mode was originally developed to address higher order / THP success rates under pressure, but its implementation has structural issues, specifically that it relies on preproducing watermark buffers in contiguous pageblocks for higher orders, which can fail under competition. The reviewer expressed hope to make defrag_mode the default eventually and acknowledged some bugs and overreclaim issues still being figured out.
review_comments.raw_body:
As you may remember it was developed to help with higher order / THP
success rates under pressure.

The impetus for actually deploying it was that we saw issues with
avalanches of large page cache folios vacuuming up the higher-order
chunks; this (ironically) also led to failures on the network side.

It's kind of a structural problem. We have real preproduction buffers
for order-0 pages through the watermarks. But for higher orders we
only ensure there is at least one page. That easily fails under even
mild competition.

Since we wanted to roll defrag_mode for THP in multi-tenant systems
anyway, we figured we might as well take the plunge now and battle
test the feature this way.

defrag_mode fixes *that* issue, by preproducing watermark buffers in
contiguous pageblocks - making everything up to that order more
readily available. I'm still hoping to make it the default eventually,
which was the plan with the original huge page allocator series. As we
keep leaning into higher order requests more and more, and especially
grow the non-optional ones, we kind of need non-optional preproduction
guarantees for higher orders as well.

But there are bugs like this one, and we're still figuring out some
overreclaim issues with it in production as well. So I'm glad it's
optional for the time being ;-)

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards
[PATCH v3 4/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards
[PATCH v4 0/4] cleanup: Remove NULL check from unconditional guards

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks from unconditional guards in the Linux kernel. The checks were dead code because the guards guarantee a non-NULL lock pointer at destructor time. The patch affects several macros, including DEFINE_GUARD(), DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(), and DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0(). It also fixes a few specific instances where the NULL check was not being used correctly. The changes are verified to have no performance impact, with a negligible reduction in code size.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()
[PATCH v4 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch annotates guard constructors in the Linux kernel with __nonnull(), which tells the compiler to warn when a NULL pointer is statically known to be passed. This is done by re-declaring the constructor with __nonnull() for DEFINE_GUARD() and directly annotating it for __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(). The __nonnull() macro is defined in compiler_attributes.h, following existing conventions. This change aims to improve code quality by catching potential NULL pointer issues at compile-time.

review_comments.author: Peter Zijlstra
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Peter Zijlstra noted that the build robot reported an issue with clang-23 being confused by the patch, and suggested that Dmitry Ilvokhin is likely CC'd on the email.
review_comments.raw_body:
The build robot found something to hate in this one. I think you're on
Cc there. It looks to me like clang-23 is confused somehow, but who
knows.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a conflict between their __nonnull() macro and glibc's own macro, proposed three possible solutions: dropping the macro, renaming it to avoid conflict, or redefining it to match glibc's convention; they are leaning towards option 1.
review_comments.raw_body:
Seems like clang is not confused here, I was able to reproduce the problem
with GCC 11 as well.

There is a conflict with glibc's own __nonnull macro

    https://elixir.bootlin.com/glibc/glibc-2.43/source/misc/sys/cdefs.h#L560-L562

which doesn't match the one from include/linux/compiler_attributes.h.
They usually don't collide, except for User Mode Linux builds, which
include both kernel and userspace headers.

Options are:

1. Drop the __nonnull() macro from include/linux/compiler_attributes.h
   and use __attribute__((__nonnull__())) directly in
   include/linux/cleanup.h. This is a bit unfortunate, since __nonnull()
   seems like a useful shortcut, but seems like the simplest solution.

2. Rename __nonnull() to __nonnull_args() to avoid the conflict.
   A returns_nonnull attribute is supported by compilers, so the name
   fits, but it diverges from the existing naming convention in
   include/linux/compiler_attributes.h.

3. #define __nonnull(params) __attribute__((__nonnull__ params)). This
   keeps the name and is compatible with both kernel and glibc usage.
   Current call sites use __nonnull() with no arguments, which works
   identically. Future callers with specific parameter numbers would use
   __nonnull((1, 2)) with double parens, matching glibc's convention. I
   don't like this option, listed it here for the sake of completeness.

I am leaning towards option 1 so far.

review_comments.author: Miguel Ojeda
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miguel Ojeda suggested standardizing the spelling of __nonnull() across the kernel by introducing a single, consistent naming convention for this attribute, such as just 'nonnull', '__knonnull', or other variations, to avoid confusion and make maintenance easier.
review_comments.raw_body:
:(

What about other similar names? i.e. a variation of your option 2,
e.g. just `nonnull` (we also have others like that, i.e. no
underscore, e.g. `noinline`), or `___nonnull` (triple underscore, but
may be confusing), or a suffix/prefix letter, e.g. `__knonnull` (for
kernel nonnull)...

i.e. it would be nice to have a "standard" spelling for ourselves, and
also replace the existing `__attribute__((nonnull))`s we have
elsewhere in the tree.

Cheers,
Miguel

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] genirq: Move NULL check into irqdesc_lock guard unlock expression

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 2/4] genirq: Move NULL check into irqdesc_lock guard unlock expression
[PATCH v4 2/4] genirq: Move NULL check into irqdesc_lock guard unlock expression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch moves a NULL check from the __DEFINE_UNLOCK_GUARD macro to the call site of irqdesc_lock's unlock expression, making the handling of NULL locks explicit and preparing for future removal of the NULL check from the macro.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class
[PATCH v4 1/4] nvdimm: Convert nvdimm_bus guard to class

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch converts the nvdimm_bus guard from using DEFINE_GUARD() to a combination of DEFINE_CLASS() and DEFINE_CLASS_IS_GUARD(). This change is necessary because the upcoming removal of NULL checks in DEFINE_GUARD() constructors would cause a compile error for nvdimm_bus, which can pass NULL. The conversion does not introduce any functional changes.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 3/4] cleanup: Annotate guard constructors with __nonnull()
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch aims to annotate guard constructors with __nonnull() to improve code clarity and consistency.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: Identified a conflict between the __nonnull() macro and glibc's own __nonnull macro. Proposed three options to resolve the issue: dropping the macro, renaming it, or defining it as an alias.

review_comments.author: Miguel Ojeda
review_comments.summary: Expressed concern about the naming convention and suggested exploring alternative spellings, such as 'nonnull' or '__knonnull', to establish a standard.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, May 26, 2026 at 5:13 PM Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
>
> They usually don't collide, except for User Mode Linux builds, which
> include both kernel and userspace headers.

:(

What about other similar names? i.e. a variation of your option 2,
e.g. just `nonnull` (we also have others like that, i.e. no
underscore, e.g. `noinline`), or `___nonnull` (triple underscore, but
may be confusing), or a suffix/prefix letter, e.g. `__knonnull` (for
kernel nonnull)...

i.e. it would be nice to have a "standard" spelling for ourselves, and
also replace the existing `__attribute__((nonnull))`s we have
elsewhere in the tree.

Cheers,
Miguel

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH RFC 3/3] mm: use non-temporal stores for demotion
Re: [PATCH 1/4] mm/mempolicy: kernel_migrate_pages: simplify the usage of put_task_struct()

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] SUNRPC: always drain cache_cleaner before destroying a cache_detail

version_history.subject: [PATCH] SUNRPC: always drain cache_cleaner before destroying a cache_detail

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the SUNRPC cache implementation by ensuring that the cache_cleaner delayed work is always cancelled before destroying a cache_detail, even when the cache_list is not empty.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified the patch as a valid fix for the use-after-free bug and applied it to nfsd-testing.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Tue, 26 May 2026 15:35:06 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> sunrpc_destroy_cache_detail() only cancels the global cache_cleaner
> delayed_work when cache_list is empty.  During per-netns teardown
> cache_list is never empty because init_net's caches remain registered,
> so the cancel never fires.  After unlink, the caller proceeds to
> cache_destroy_net() which kfrees the cache_detail while cache_clean()
> may still hold a dangling pointer to it.  The result is a
> use-after-free: cache_dequeue() takes cd->queue_lock on freed memory,
> and cache_put() dereferences cd->cache_put as a function pointer from
> freed slab.
> 
> [...]

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/1] SUNRPC: always drain cache_cleaner before destroying a cache_detail
      commit: a6a67f4010de2424ae856d5e857079fe89f1178d

--
Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/2] nfsd: callback channel fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/2] nfsd: defer setting NFSD4_CALLBACK_RUNNING in deleg_reaper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS daemon where the NFSD4_CALLBACK_RUNNING bit is set too early, preventing recall-any dispatch for affected clients. The fix involves moving the test_and_set_bit() operation to after checking the rate limit and callback state gates. This ensures that the bit is only taken when nfsd4_run_cb() will be called.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/2] nfsd: clear CALLBACK_RUNNING on failed delegation recall queue
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS daemon where a callback is left running even after a recall queue attempt fails, causing subsequent break_lease operations to silently skip the recall and resulting in stalled conflicting opens or locks. The fix involves clearing the CALLBACK_RUNNING bit when the recall queue attempt fails, ensuring that the delegation can be broken as expected.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch series addresses issues in the NFSd callback channel machinery, specifically deferring the setting of NFSD4_CALLBACK_RUNNING in deleg_reaper and clearing CALLBACK_RUNNING on failed delegation recall queue.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever

> Some fixes for the callback channel machinery. These problems were
> mostly found via agentic scans.

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/2] nfsd: defer setting NFSD4_CALLBACK_RUNNING in deleg_reaper
      commit: 02b9bb6ab68a77b1112d48411e4e08308a077ea0
[2/2] nfsd: clear CALLBACK_RUNNING on failed delegation recall queue

Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] nfsd: don't free session slots that are still in use

version_history.subject: [PATCH] nfsd: don't free session slots that are still in use
[PATCH v2] nfsd: don't free session slots that are still in use

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a use-after-free vulnerability in the NFSv4 server by adding two checks to prevent freeing session slots that are still in use. The first check ensures that the current request's slotid is below the shrink boundary, and the second scan checks for active slots in the range to be freed.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified the issue and suggested a fix. The patch addresses both problems: checking slotid against se_target_maxslots, and scanning slots in the to-be-freed range for NFSD4_SLOT_INUSE.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>

On Tue, 26 May 2026 12:24:48 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> nfsd4_sequence() can free the very slot it is currently processing.
> When the session shrinker has reduced se_target_maxslots below
> se_fchannel.maxreqs, the shrink path checks three conditions before
> calling free_session_slots():
> 
>   1. se_target_maxslots < maxreqs  (shrink was advertised)
>   2. slot->sl_generation == se_slot_gen  (slot is up-to-date)
>   3. seq->maxslots <= se_target_maxslots  (client acknowledges)
> 
> [...]

Applied to nfsd-testing, thanks!

[1/1] nfsd: don't free session slots that are still in use
      commit: 6085718398d83e16ccdc864c209493671f3c5998

--
Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>
